title: Org Agent Config — Coding Agent: It's Alive!

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this challenge lab you will configure the GitHub Coding Agent for an organization and repository, craft issues the agent can execute successfully, and build handoff workflows between your IDE and the cloud agent.

Your mission: produce a complete Coding Agent configuration package that a teammate could drop into a real repository and start using immediately. The slides teach the concepts; the lab challenges you to apply them.

Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Compare the Two Agent Experiences
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask it to summarize the difference between the Coding Agent and VS Code agent mode.

What is the difference between the GitHub Coding Agent and VS Code agent mode? When should I use each one?

Challenge: Based on the response and the slide, identify at least three scenarios where the Coding Agent is the better choice, and three where agent mode wins.
steps.tip: Think about async vs. interactive, access to local tools vs. cloud isolation, and fire-and-forget vs. real-time guidance.

steps.title: Map Your Team's Workflow
steps.instruction:
Open a new file and sketch out where each agent experience fits in your team's development flow. Consider which tasks you would delegate to the Coding Agent overnight and which you would tackle interactively in VS Code.

This mental model will guide the configuration work ahead.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Trace the Agent Lifecycle
steps.instruction:
The Coding Agent follows a six-step lifecycle. In your open file, write a brief outline of each step in your own words: issue assignment, environment setup, planning, implementation, pull request, and review loop.

Challenge: For each step, note what you control (inputs, configuration) versus what the agent decides on its own. This distinction drives your entire configuration strategy.

steps.title: Identify Your Configuration Surface
steps.instruction:
Look at your lifecycle outline. Three configuration files shape the agent's behavior:

• copilot-instructions.md (organization level)
• copilot-instructions.md (repository level)
• copilot-setup-steps.yml

Mark which lifecycle step each file influences. Keep this as your checklist for the rest of the lab.

steps.title: Locate the Configuration Directory
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer and look for a .github/ folder in the project. If one exists, check whether copilot-instructions.md is already present.

Challenge: What other configuration files live in .github/? How would the Coding Agent interact with them?

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E

steps.title: Draft an Agent-Ready Issue
steps.instruction:
Open a new file and draft a GitHub Issue description for a realistic task you would assign to the Coding Agent. Choose something concrete: adding a REST endpoint, writing tests for an existing module, or updating documentation.

Your issue needs a clear title, the desired outcome, acceptance criteria, and references to specific files or functions.

steps.title: Stress-Test Your Issue
steps.instruction:
Paste your issue into Copilot Chat and ask it to evaluate the description:

Review this GitHub Issue description as if you were the Coding Agent. Do you have enough context to implement it? What ambiguities would cause problems?

Refine the issue based on the feedback.

steps.title: Apply the Scoping Test
steps.instruction:
Challenge: Does your issue pass the single-task test? If Copilot suggested splitting it into multiple issues, that is a strong signal it is too broad. The Coding Agent works best on focused, well-defined changes.

Tighten the scope until the issue represents exactly one coherent piece of work.
steps.tip: A good rule of thumb: if the acceptance criteria list has more than five items, the issue probably covers too much ground.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Design Org-Level Instructions
steps.instruction:
Create a new file. This will be your organization-level copilot-instructions.md, the file that applies to every repository in your org.

Challenge: Write instructions covering universal standards. Think about:
• Default language and framework preferences
• Security rules (secrets, queries, input validation)
• Testing requirements
• Pull request conventions

Be specific. "Write clean code" is not actionable. "Always include unit tests for new functions" is.

steps.title: Audit for Specificity
steps.instruction:
Paste your org-level instructions into Copilot Chat and ask:

Review these organization-level Copilot instructions for the Coding Agent. Are they specific enough for an autonomous agent to follow literally? Flag any vague or ambiguous guidance.

The agent does not infer intent. Every instruction must be unambiguous.
steps.tip: Great org instructions are short and universal. If a rule only applies to some projects, it belongs at the repo level instead.

steps.title: Test the Security Section
steps.instruction:
Challenge: Does your instructions file include security rules? If not, add them now. At minimum, consider:
• No hardcoded secrets or API keys
• Parameterized queries for database access
• Input validation at system boundaries

These are the kind of universal policies that prevent the agent from introducing vulnerabilities across any repo.

steps.title: Check Extension Output
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel and select GitHub Copilot from the dropdown. The extension logs show how Copilot processes your instruction files.

Challenge: Can you spot where your instruction files are loaded? This log is invaluable when debugging why instructions are not being followed.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Design Repo-Level Instructions
steps.instruction:
Create another new file for repository-level copilot-instructions.md. Pick a real or hypothetical project and write instructions that specialize the org-level baseline.

Challenge: Cover at least four areas:
• Framework and architecture patterns
• File naming and placement conventions
• Testing framework and file locations
• Styling or documentation standards

These rules should make sense only for this project, not your whole org.

steps.title: Check for Layer Conflicts
steps.instruction:
Open your org-level and repo-level instructions side by side. The agent merges all instruction layers additively.

Challenge: Do any of your rules conflict? If your org says "use Jest" but your repo says "use Vitest," the agent's behavior is unpredictable. Find and resolve any contradictions.

The principle: org instructions set the baseline; repo instructions specialize, not override.
steps.tip: If different repos need different testing tools, make the org instruction flexible: "use the project's configured test framework" rather than naming a specific tool.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Create the Setup Steps File
steps.instruction:
Create a new file named copilot-setup-steps.yml. This file uses GitHub Actions step syntax to tell the agent how to prepare the build environment.

Challenge: Write setup steps for the same project you used in your repo-level instructions. Without this file, the agent cannot build, test, or verify its own work.

steps.title: Include the Essentials
steps.instruction:
Your setup steps should cover four things:

• Language/runtime setup (e.g., actions/setup-node@v4)
• Dependency installation
• Build verification
• Test suite execution

Challenge: Can the agent run your test suite after making changes? If not, it is coding blind. Make sure npm test, make test, or your equivalent is in the steps.

steps.title: Optimize for Speed
steps.instruction:
Every second of setup time adds to the agent's turnaround. Review your steps for performance.

Ask Copilot Chat:

Review this copilot-setup-steps.yml file. How can I make the setup faster without losing the ability to build and test?

Common wins: use npm ci over npm install, skip heavy compilation steps that are not needed for testing, and avoid installing tools the agent will never use.

steps.title: Test Your Setup Steps Locally
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and try running the commands from your setup steps file. Do they work? Does the test suite pass?

Challenge: The Coding Agent's environment mirrors a fresh GitHub Actions runner. If your commands depend on local state, the agent will fail. Identify any assumptions baked into your steps.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Build a PR Review Checklist
steps.instruction:
The agent opens a pull request. You review it. But what should you look for in agent-generated code specifically?

Open a new file and create a review checklist for Coding Agent PRs. Consider:
• Does the code solve the issue's acceptance criteria?
• Does it follow your instruction files?
• Are tests present and meaningful, not just boilerplate?
• Are there security concerns?
• Did it handle edge cases or just the happy path?

steps.title: Practice Writing Review Comments
steps.instruction:
When you leave comments on an agent PR, the agent reads them and pushes follow-up commits. The quality of your comments directly affects the quality of the fix.

Challenge: Write three example review comments in your file. Each one should be specific, actionable, and scoped to a single concern.

Good: "This endpoint should return 400 for invalid query parameters, not 500. Add input validation before the database call."
Weak: "The error handling needs improvement."
steps.tip: If the agent's approach is fundamentally wrong, close the PR and refine the issue instead of trying to fix it through review comments.

steps.title: Preview Changes in Source Control
steps.instruction:
Open the Source Control panel. This is the same diff view you would use to review a Coding Agent PR locally after checking out its branch.

Challenge: Practice reading diffs critically. What patterns signal that agent-generated code needs closer review?

steps.title: Search for Agent Commands
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type Copilot to explore available Copilot commands. Note the ones related to agent configuration and instructions.

Challenge: Which commands would help you debug why the Coding Agent is not following your instructions?

steps.title: Spot the Strengths and Risks
steps.instruction:
The slide identifies what the agent does well and where it needs extra scrutiny. Challenge: For the task in your sample issue, predict which parts the agent will handle confidently and which parts you should review line by line.

Write your predictions in the file. When you try the agent for real, compare your predictions to the actual output.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P

steps.title: Plan an IDE-to-Agent Handoff
steps.instruction:
You have been prototyping a feature in VS Code agent mode. Now you want to hand it off to the Coding Agent to finish.

Open Copilot Chat and ask it to help you write a handoff issue:

I've been exploring a feature locally. Help me write a detailed GitHub Issue that captures my findings so the Coding Agent can complete the implementation. The feature is: [describe a realistic feature].

steps.title: Review Agent Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for copilot. Explore the configuration options that affect how Copilot Chat and the Coding Agent interact with your workspace.

Challenge: Which settings would you change before handing off work to the Coding Agent versus working interactively in agent mode?

steps.title: Plan an Agent-to-IDE Handoff
steps.instruction:
Now consider the reverse: the Coding Agent opened a PR, and you want to refine it locally in VS Code.

Challenge: Write down a step-by-step handoff checklist covering:
• How you check out the agent's branch
• How you use VS Code agent mode to refine the code
• How you push refinements back to the same PR
• When you would use Edit mode vs. Agent mode for the refinements

steps.title: Simulate a Branch Checkout
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run git branch -a to see all available branches. In a real handoff, you would check out the agent's branch with git checkout copilot/fix-... and review its work locally.

Challenge: What is the first thing you would check after switching to the agent's branch?

steps.title: Choose Your Workflow Pattern
steps.instruction:
You now know two handoff directions: IDE-to-Agent and Agent-to-IDE. In practice, which pattern will you use more?

Challenge: For a typical week of work, identify two tasks you would start locally and hand off, and two tasks you would assign directly to the agent. Write them down with your reasoning.
steps.tip: The "explore locally, delegate implementation" pattern works best for tasks that are conceptually complex but mechanically straightforward.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Estimate Capacity Needs
steps.instruction:
The Coding Agent consumes GitHub Actions minutes. For your team's expected usage, estimate the monthly cost.

Ask Copilot Chat:

If a team of 10 developers assigns an average of 4 issues per week to the Coding Agent, and each session uses roughly 15 minutes of Actions time, what is the monthly Actions minute consumption?

Challenge: How does this compare to your current Actions usage? Is the ROI worth it?

steps.title: Define Access Controls
steps.instruction:
Organization admins can enable or disable the Coding Agent at the org and repository level.

Challenge: For your organization, draft a policy for which repositories should have the agent enabled and which should not. Consider:
• Security-sensitive repositories with strict change controls
• Repositories with complex builds that would slow the agent down
• Team readiness and review bandwidth for agent PRs
• Repositories where the agent would provide the most value

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Assemble Your Configuration Package
steps.instruction:
Review everything you have built during this lab. Your complete Coding Agent configuration package should include:

• Organization-level copilot-instructions.md
• Repository-level copilot-instructions.md
• copilot-setup-steps.yml with fast, complete setup steps
• A well-crafted sample issue with acceptance criteria
• A PR review checklist for agent-generated code
• A handoff workflow plan (both directions)
• A governance policy for access and capacity

steps.title: Review Your File Structure
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer and verify the files you have created. A complete configuration package should show .github/copilot-instructions.md and .github/copilot-setup-steps.yml in the tree.

Challenge: Is the file layout clean enough for a teammate to understand immediately?

steps.title: Final Quality Check
steps.instruction:
Challenge: Could a teammate pick up your configuration files and start using the Coding Agent effectively today? Open Copilot Chat and ask:

I created a Coding Agent configuration package with org instructions, repo instructions, and setup steps. What is missing that could cause problems for a new team adopting the agent?

Fill any gaps the review identifies.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! You have completed Coding Agent: It's Alive!

You built a complete Coding Agent configuration package:
• Organization and repository-level custom instructions
• Environment setup steps for the agent's build environment
• Agent-ready issue descriptions with acceptance criteria
• A PR review checklist and actionable comment patterns
• Handoff workflows in both directions (IDE to agent and agent to IDE)
• A governance and capacity plan

Next steps: try assigning a real issue to the Coding Agent in one of your repositories. Then explore Agent Potato: License to Chat for a deep dive into VS Code agent mode, or Enterprise-Grade Marmalade for scaling Copilot across large organizations.
